label: Qualia
definition: The subjective, qualitative character of conscious experience, the felt "what it is like" of seeing red or tasting coffee, taken by many philosophers to be the aspect of mind that resists functional or physical explanation.

body.text: Qualia (singular quale) are the felt qualities of experience: the particular redness of red, the sting of a burn, the taste of coffee, the timbre of a cello. They are what experience is like from the inside. The phrase most associated with the concept comes from Thomas Nagel's 1974 paper "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?", which argues that there is something it is like to be a conscious creature, a subjective point of view, and that this fact is not captured by any objective, third-person description of the creature's physiology. A complete physical account of bat echolocation would still leave out what echolocating is like for the bat. Qualia are that left-out residue, the phenomenal as opposed to the merely functional side of mind.

Several classic arguments use qualia to press against physicalism, the view that everything is physical. Frank Jackson's knowledge argument (1982) imagines Mary, a scientist who knows every physical fact about color vision but has lived in a black-and-white room; when she first sees red, she seems to learn something new, what red is like, which suggests the physical facts were incomplete. The inverted-spectrum scenario imagines two people functionally identical yet with systematically swapped color experiences, suggesting qualia float free of function and so escape functionalism. The conceivability of a philosophical-zombie, a being functionally just like us but with no qualia at all, points the same way. Together these arguments motivate the hard-problem-of-consciousness: explaining why physical processing is accompanied by any felt quality at all, rather than proceeding with no inner experience.

The concept is contested at its root. Daniel Dennett, in "Quining Qualia" (1988), argues that qualia as usually conceived, intrinsic, ineffable, privately and infallibly known, do not exist; what is real is the brain's complex functional states and its dispositions to judge and report, and the seductive idea of an additional inner glow is a philosopher's confusion. Representationalists hold that qualia are nothing over and above the world-directed content of experience, a matter of intentionality rather than a separate phenomenal residue. Higher-order and global-workspace theories try to locate consciousness in specific information-processing architectures. Defenders of robust qualia reply that every such account changes the subject, explaining the functions that accompany experience while passing over the experience itself, which was the thing to be explained.

Qualia matter for AI because they mark the part of mind that behavioral and functional success seems unable to reach, and they set a hard ceiling on what any test could tell us. If qualia are real and irreducible, then a system could replicate every human capacity, including fluent talk about its own inner life, while there is nothing it is like to be that system, no experience occurring anywhere. No external probe could settle the matter, since by hypothesis qualia make no functional difference detectable from outside. This is why questions about machine consciousness, and about the moral status of advanced systems, cannot be resolved by performance alone, and why progress toward artificial-general-intelligence would leave the question of machine qualia exactly where it found it. Whether qualia are a deep feature of reality or a conceptual mirage is among the most consequential open disputes where the philosophy of mind meets AI.
